name: Soda-Lime Glass Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Soda-Lime Glass. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Soda-Lime Glass surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength optimized for Soda-Lime Glass absorption and minimal subsurface damage

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning with adequate energy density

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage and thermal management

scanSpeed.description: Scan velocity for effective cleaning with controlled thermal input

power.description: Typical range for 1064 nm pulsed lasers in industrial cleaning of delicate substrates like soda-lime glass to remove contaminants without thermal damage; starting at 100 W for controlled ablation. Max limited to avoid heat accumulation given glass's low thermal conductivity (~1 W/m·K). Source: Manufacturer specs from IPG Photonics and Coherent for YAG lasers; academic review in 'Laser Cleaning' by Kononenko et al. (Optics and Lasers in Engineering, 2016).

scan_speed.description: Effective range for contaminant removal on glass surfaces; slower speeds (e.g., 500 mm/s) for heavy contamination like oils/paints, faster (up to 3000 mm/s) for light dust. Recommended 1500 mm/s balances efficiency and minimal heat input. Source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Europe guidelines and equipment manuals (e.g., Adapt Laser Systems); case studies in Journal of Cultural Heritage (2018) for glass artifacts.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime (5-100 ns) preferred for cost-effective cleaning with sufficient peak power for contaminant ejection; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) reduce heat-affected zone (HAZ) but are overkill for most industrial glass cleaning due to transparency at 1064 nm. Recommended 20 ns minimizes thermal diffusion in glass (melting point ~500°C). Source: Laser physics research in Applied Surface Science (e.g., Palmieri et al., 2019) on ns vs. ps for dielectrics.

repetition_rate.description: Typical 10-100 kHz for pulsed cleaning; higher rates speed up process but risk heat accumulation on low-conductivity glass (reflectivity ~4% at 1064 nm). Recommended 50 kHz for optimal balance in contaminant removal without substrate heating. Source: Academic literature in Optics Express (e.g., Anisimov et al., 2015) on repetition rate effects in laser ablation of glasses.

energy_density.description: Fluence range for safe cleaning of contaminants; damage threshold for soda-lime glass at 1064 nm ns pulses is ~10-15 J/cm², so stay below 2 J/cm². Recommended 0.5 J/cm² for effective removal of industrial contaminants like rust/oils without cracking. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (e.g., Stuart et al., 1996) and Laser & Particle Beams (2017) for IR cleaning of dielectrics.

spot_size.description: Beam diameter range for precision cleaning; smaller spots (100 μm) for fine details on glass, larger (500 μm) for broad areas. Recommended 200 μm suits industrial surface preparation on soda-lime glass to ensure uniform fluence. Source: Equipment specifications from Trumpf and CleanLaser systems; process studies in Applied Optics (2020).

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, up to 5 for heavy layers; multi-pass preferred for glass to distribute energy and avoid damage. Recommended 2 passes for complete removal in industrial settings. Source: Industry practice from case studies in Surface and Coatings Technology (e.g., 2014 paper on glass cleaning) and manufacturer guidelines (e.g., Lasertec).

overlap_ratio.description: Typical 30-80% for uniform coverage without gaps or over-exposure; 50% recommended for soda-lime glass to ensure even cleaning while minimizing total energy input and potential thermal stress. Source: Process optimization studies in Lasers in Manufacturing and Materials Processing (2019) and industry standards from SPIE proceedings on scan strategies.

material_characteristics.label: Material Characteristics
material_characteristics.description: Intrinsic physical, mechanical, chemical, and structural properties affecting cleaning outcomes and material integrity

oxidationResistance.unit: dimensionless (normalized scale 0-1, where 1 indicates complete resistance)

laserDamageThreshold.source_name: Laser damage threshold measurements of commercial glasses at 1064 nm
laserDamageThreshold.citation: Gleason, R. E., et al., Applied Optics, Vol. 35, Issue 9, pp. 1439-1444, 1996, DOI: 10.1364/AO.35.001439
laserDamageThreshold.context: Commercial soda-lime float glass (72% SiO2, 14% Na2O, 9% CaO, typical window glass composition), room temperature (25°C), 1064 nm Nd:YAG laser, 10 ns pulse duration, single-pulse (1-on-1) damage testing

laser_material_interaction.label: Laser-Material Interaction
laser_material_interaction.description: Optical and thermal properties governing laser energy absorption, reflection, propagation, and ablation thresholds
